relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Sacrifice may not disguise neglect or contempt.
Covenant violation becomes a matter for public inquiry.
The accusation concerns worship of another power or the heavens.
Rumor must become diligent investigation before judgment.
The ancient law nevertheless answers religious deviation with death.
One witness is not enough to take a life.
Witnesses bear the first burden of the sentence they seek.
Hard cases may be carried beyond the local gate.
Priest and judge together receive the appeal.
The central judgment is treated as binding.
Interpretation itself becomes part of Torah's authority.
The text answers defiance of that court with death.
Fear is expected to stabilize the legal order.
Israel anticipates wanting a king like surrounding nations.
Kingship is permitted but not left without covenant limits.
The king may not build military power by returning people toward Egypt.
He may not accumulate wives, silver, or gold around himself.
He must personally copy Torah rather than merely possess advisers.
Daily reading keeps power beneath instruction.
The ruler's heart must not rise above his brothers.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 17 is suspicious of power even while permitting it. The king is forbidden the classic tools of empire—war horses, sexual accumulation, and excess wealth—and must read Torah until he remembers he is still a brother, not a god.
